title: Pastoral Power: Perspectives on the Present
author: Stypinska, Rossi

opening_move.description: As a special section introduction, the paper opens by situating pastoral power scholarship and diagnosing a gap: existing Foucauldian literature either focuses on exegesis of Foucault's texts or subsumes pastoral power under the broader study of governmentality, thereby diluting its specificity — particularly its ethical, ascetic, theological and veridictional dimensions. The paper argues these overlooked dimensions are precisely what makes pastoral power analytically valuable for understanding contemporary power formations.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Pastoral power continues to directly animate governmental techniques across neoliberal and neo-authoritarian contexts (digital platforms, self-optimization, debt subjectivity, ecological apocalyptics), and only by attending to its theological specificity — not just its secular governmental forms — can we understand these contemporary formations.

theoretical_positioning.description: The paper performs immanent critique of Foucauldian governmentality studies — accepting the pastoral power concept while arguing that its appropriation by governmentality studies has erased its ethical, veridictional and theological specificity, reducing it to a secular administrative paradigm. The paper advocates restoring the theological dimension (truth-subjectivity triangulation) while situating it in productive dialogue with political and economic theology (Agamben, Stimilli, Esposito).

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: As a special section introduction, the paper reconstructs the Foucauldian pastoral power concept, maps its appropriation in governmentality studies, diagnoses a gap, outlines two problematiques (truth-subjectivity and theological subtexts), and previews the contributions of the other articles. No original empirical or interpretive research is performed.
epistemic_mode.notes: This is a special section introduction — same genre as seeliger-sevignani-2022 — and the epistemic mode is therefore fully_normative. The contemporary examples cited (algorithms, self-optimization discourse, digital influencing) function as symptoms that motivate the theoretical inquiry rather than as empirical evidence for specific claims. The paper's intellectual work is entirely conceptual: reconstructing the pastoral concept, critiquing its assimilation into governmentality studies, and framing the special section's two problematiques.

confidence_notes: Like seeliger-sevignani-2022, this is a special section introduction functioning primarily as an orienting and framing text rather than an original argument. The immanent_critique target is the governmentality studies tradition, which the paper argues has domesticated Foucault's pastoral power by treating it as secular administration and losing its theological-ethical specificity. The normative stake is medium rather than high because, as an introduction, the paper does not itself argue for specific normative conclusions — it frames the problematiques that the other articles develop. The framework_proposal is the two-problematique structure (truth-subjectivity triangulation + theological subtexts) that organizes the special section. The move_sequence reflects the genre: genealogical_reconstruction of Foucault's pastoral power analysis → immanent_critique of how governmentality studies has assimilated it → conceptual_distinction between pastoral power's theological-veridictional dimension and its governmentality-studies reduction → framework_proposal of the two problematiques → conclusion_open as research agenda for the special section.
